message:
refactor(ffs-repair/ownership): extract acquire_conflicting helpers and verify post-rewrite claim

The single large function in acquire_conflicting handled four distinct
concerns (reading+deserializing the record, deciding whether we already
owned the claim, deciding whether to rewrite on conflict, and handling
the race-loss path) inline with overlapping bookkeeping. Extracting
them makes each decision explicit and testable, and lets the same
claim-identity predicate run both before and after a rewrite.

Changes:
- Extract `read_record` so the double read (pre-check, and re-read after
  a rewrite to observe the final committed claim) no longer duplicates
  the file read + JSON deserialize + error-conversion plumbing.
- Extract `claim_matches(record, gen, version)` so the ownership
  identity check is defined once and compares all three coordinates
  (host_id, repair_generation, lease_version) instead of only
  host_id. Previously, a stale record that still bore our host_id but
  had a different generation or lease_version would be accepted as
  ours, which is unsafe under lease rotation and repair-generation
  bumps.
- Extract `should_rewrite_conflicting_claim` to make the tiebreak
  explicit: rewrite when we see a strictly older generation, or when
  the generation matches and our host_id sorts lower (the original
  lexicographic tiebreak).
- Extract `acquisition_result_for_current_claim` so after a successful
  rewrite we re-read and re-verify before declaring ownership. If the
  post-rewrite state doesn't still satisfy `claim_matches`, we log
  and return ConflictLost with the observed/expected generations and
  lease_versions (new `ownership_claim_verification_lost` event), which
  closes the window where a competitor's concurrent rewrite between
  our write and re-read would have been silently treated as our own.
- Extract `conflict_lost` for the plain race-loss path and enrich the
  existing `ownership_conflict_lost` log with `winner_generation` so
  operators can tell from logs alone which generation the winner was
  at.

Behavior is unchanged for the common cases (we own it; we rewrite and
win; we lose the race outright); the change tightens the "we rewrote
but someone rewrote again underneath us" case from silently succeeding
to explicitly yielding ConflictLost.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
